content: test: memory system is operational — validation entry

Common pitfall: Shared package dist/ staleness. When modifying a shared package (e.g., @cfxdevkit/cas-shared), dependents typecheck against old dist/. Fix: pnpm --filter @cfxdevkit/cas-shared build before typechecking dependents.

Common pitfall: Moon project IDs differ from pnpm filter names. Use pnpm --filter @cfxdevkit/<package> (from package.json), NOT moon run <project-id>:task. Example: pnpm --filter @cfxdevkit/cas-backend test is correct; moon run cas-backend:test may fail if Moon project ID doesn't exist.
Common pitfall: Biome import ordering enforced. After adding imports, run: pnpm exec biome check --write <file> or pnpm --filter <pkg> lint to auto-fix.

Common pitfall: TypeScript unknown type guards. When function accepts unknown and does numeric comparisons, add explicit guard: typeof value === 'number' && value >= 0. TS18046 error on bare comparisons.

Fix: gitnexus not on PATH warning was caused by Dockerfile not installing gitnexus globally. Fixed by adding `npm i -g gitnexus --prefix /home/node/.npm-global` alongside pi-coding-agent install. run.cjs is a gitignored workaround auto-generated by `gitnexus analyze` (fallback resolver: PATH → pnpm dlx → npx), NOT the primary invocation. The real fix is global npm install so pi-gitnexus extension finds gitnexus via probeGitNexusBinary().

Critical: Never use getModel("openai-compat", modelId) from @earendil-works/pi-ai/compat — it's aliased to getBuiltinModel() which ONLY searches hardcoded pi-ai builtin providers (anthropic, openai, etc.). "openai-compat" is a CUSTOM provider registered dynamically by bridge.ts via model-registry.js. Always use modelRegistry.find("openai-compat", modelId) from the ExtensionAPI's modelRegistry instead. This affects memory.ts (reflection and extraction) and any code that needs to resolve custom providers.
High session length variance (2–166 messages) reflects inconsistent task scoping. Standardize initial prompts with clear boundaries to reduce fragmented sessions.

Repeated sessions ending at 2 and 5 messages suggest premature context abandonment or unnecessary resets. Investigate whether these are failed attempts or highly efficient micro-tasks to prevent workflow fragmentation.

Sessions terminating with exactly 2 messages occurred 3 times, indicating a recurring pattern of premature closure or missing context. Always include explicit goals, constraints, and expected output format in the initial prompt to prevent early drops.
